pages.text:
CHAPTER 4—SPECIAL DETAILED REQUIREMENTS BASED ON OCCUPANCY and 
use 
 
 
SBC 201-CC-2024 
357 
 
the provisions of the code. Incorporating 
intervening rooms and/or aisles is also another 
option for providing compliant exit access 
means of egress within Group I-1. For 
example, this can occur in assisted living 
memory care facilities where sleeping units 
open directly into dining, multipurpose, and 
therapy/activity rooms. 
 
It is often desirable to have the shared living, 
group meeting, multipurpose, and therapeutic 
areas in assisted living and assisted living 
memory care facilities open to exit access 
corridors. The list is intended to note generic 
spaces, not specific rooms or spaces. Typical 
shared living areas can include dining, TV, 
activity, and family rooms or similar spaces. 
This can create a more residential, homelike 
environment that can enhance the socialization 
of the resident care recipients. The code 
recognizes this and considers the potential care 
level needed and the 24-hour staffing while 
requiring enhanced protective safeguards in 
these areas. The code permits these areas to 
be open to corridors by including compensating 
protection 
features 
(e.g., 
sprinklers, 
arrangement, corridor construction, automatic 
fire detection and quick-response sprinklers). 
420.8  Group I-1 cooking facilities.  In Group I-
1 occupancies, rooms or spaces that contain a 
cooking facility with domestic cooking appliances 
shall be permitted to be open to the corridor where 
all of the following criteria are met: 
1 
In Group I-1, Condition 1 occupancies, the 
number of care recipients served by one 
cooking facility shall not be greater than 30. 
2 
In Group I-1, Condition 2 occupancies, the 
number of care recipients served by one 
cooking facility and within the same smoke 
compartment shall not be greater than 30. 
3 
The space containing the cooking facilities 
shall be arranged so as not to obstruct access 
to the required exit. 
4 
The cooking appliances shall comply with 
Section 420.9 . 
❖ 
This section allows smaller cooking spaces in 
more open spaces similar to what Group I-2 in 
Section 407.2 allows, while also incorporating 
similar safeguards. See also the commentary 
to Section 407.2.6 for further description of 
resident preferences and outcomes for open 
kitchens. 
 
Providing smaller controlled open kitchen areas 
in facilities like assisted living and assisted 
living memory care facilities also relates to the 
commentary to Section 420.7 for preferred 
homelike 
residential 
design. 
The 
code 
recognizes these preferences and considers 
the care recipient relative care level, and the 
24-hour basis care staffing, while requiring 
enhanced protective safeguards in these 
smaller kitchen areas. The code permits these 
open kitchen areas by including compensating 
protection features (e.g., sprinklers, size limits, 
arrangement, limitation of types of appliances, 
shutoff and timer controls, and extinguishing 
requirements). 
420.9  Domestic cooking appliances. In Group I-
1 occupancies, installation of cooking appliance 
used in domestic cooking facilities shall comply 
with all of the following: 
1 
The types of cooking appliances permitted 
shall be limited to ovens, cooktops, ranges, 
warmers and microwaves. 
2 
Domestic cooking hoods installed and 
constructed in accordance with Section 505 
of SBC 501 shall be provided over cooktops 
or ranges. 
3 
Cooktops and ranges shall be protected in 
accordance with Section 904.14. 
4 
A shutoff for the fuel and electrical supply to 
the cooking equipment shall be provided in a 
location to which only staff has access. 
5 
A timer shall be provided that automatically 
deactivates the cooking appliances within a 
period of not more than 120 minutes. 
6 
A portable fire extinguisher shall be 
provided. Installation shall be in accordance 
with Section 906 and the extinguisher shall 
be located within a 9 m distance of travel 
from each domestic cooking appliance. 
Exceptions: 
1 
Cooking facilities provided within care 
recipients’ individual dwelling units are not 
required to comply with this Section. 
2 
Cooktops and ranges used for care-recipient 
training or nutritional counseling are not 
required to comply with Item 3 of this 
Section. 
❖ 
When cooking for a maximum of 30 care 
recipients in each cooking facility (as described 
in Section 420.8 ), the combination of these 
safety measures and standard safety aspects 
of Group I-1 occupancies such as sprinklers, 
trained staff and rated corridor walls helps 
reduce the level of risk to an acceptable level 
-

pages.text:
CHAPTER 4—SPECIAL DETAILED REQUIREMENTS BASED ON OCCUPANCY and 
use 
 
 
SBC 201-CC-2024 
358 
 
while providing a real social and emotional 
benefit to care recipients. 
 
The appliances that are permitted to be in a 
Group I-1 cooking facility are electric or gas 
ranges, cooktops, stoves, ovens, warmers and 
microwaves. Toasters and toaster ovens fall 
into the warmer or oven category. Deep-fat 
fryers, charcoal grills, wood-burning ovens or 
commercial 
cooking 
appliances 
are 
not 
permitted in domestic cooking facilities. Only 
the cooktops and ranges are required to have a 
domestic cooking hood. 
 
Section 
904.14 
requires 
either 
a 
fire-
extinguishing system, listed and labeled to UL 
300A, to be built into the range hood or a listed 
sensor in the burner to initiate shutoff upon 
high-heat detection, thereby preventing ignition 
of a cooking fire. The built-in fire extinguishing 
system in the hood also provides for a manual 
actuation of the extinguishers and an interlock 
that shuts down the power or fuel source to the 
appliance upon actuation of the extinguisher 
heads. 
 
The cooktop or range must have a shutoff 
switch that deactivates the appliance and is 
accessible only to staff. This may be a switch 
inside a locked cabinet, a keyed switch or an 
electronic switch with pass code or similar 
security. It may be located on the wall near the 
appliance, in a kitchen cabinet or inside the 
pantry or other room. The intent is that the 
cooktop or range is only able to be used when 
staff are aware that it is being used or if staff is 
able to supervise. Similarly, a timer is required 
to deactivate the cooktop, range or oven after 
120 minutes in case a staff member was 
distracted by, or tending to, an urgent care 
situation. The timer should be able to be reset 
by the staff member, as needed, when cooking 
something that requires a cooking time of 
longer than 120 minutes. 
 
A portable fire extinguisher must be located 
within 9 m of each cooking appliance. The 
intent is that a staff member can quickly reach 
it in the event of an emergency. Ideally, it would 
be located in the same room as the cooking 
appliance but can be located outside the room 
as long as the travel distance does not exceed 
9 m. It is best practice that this be a Class K fire 
extinguisher. 
 
Exception 1: While the Section deals with a 
shared kitchen facility, some assisted living 
facilities will have kitchens within individual 
suites. These would be addressed the same as 
a standard dwelling-unit kitchen. 
 
Exception 2: Cooktops and ranges are often 
used for occupational therapy where staff is 
helping care recipients learn how to safely cook 
for themselves. Staff also teach care recipients 
how to make healthier meals to address 
conditions such as heart disease or diabetes. In 
this instance, safeguards such as shutoff 
switches and fire extinguishers are prudent, but 
the upgraded hood is not needed. The space 
containing these cooktops and ranges would 
still need to comply with SBC 501 for 
ventilation. 
420.10  Group R cooking facilities.  In Group R 
occupancies, cooking appliances used for domestic 
cooking operations shall be in accordance with 
Section 917.2 of SBC 501. 
❖ 
With Sections 420.7 and 420.8 addressing 
cooking facilities in Group I occupancies, this 
section 
addresses 
the 
balance 
of 
the 
occupancies covered by Section 420 the 
Group R occupancies. This section provides 
direction to SBC 501 provisions regarding 
domestic cooking. The intent is for all Group R 
occupancies, including dwelling units, hotel 
rooms, assisted living suites, lodging houses 
and congregate residences, to be allowed to 
use the provisions in SBC 501 for domestic 
cooking appliances. If the hotel or dormitory 
has a central restaurant or cafeteria, this 
section would not be applicable because it 
would be commercial cooking. See Section 
420.11 for cooking facilities in dormitories. 
420.11  Group R-2 dormitory cooking facilities.  
Domestic cooking appliances for use by residents of 
Group R-2 college dormitories shall be in 
accordance with Sections 420.11.1 and 420.11.2 . 
❖ 
This section provides basic requirements for 
the building official to follow in approving such 
cooking appliance installations. Item 2 of 
Section 420.11.1 allows cooking appliances in 
approved locations. Such approval is a function 
of the building official. The intent of the section 
is to allow such installations, not to prohibit 
them. The following sections address the use 
of domestic cooking appliances in both 
common areas and sleeping rooms on college 
dormitories. It does not cover resident dwelling 
units on college campuses that are not 
classified as dormitories. The provisions are 
similar to those that allow cooking in Group I-2 
facilities in Section 407.2 . See also Section 
420.11.2 . 
420.11.1  
Cooking appliances.  Where 
located in Group R-2 college dormitories, domestic 
cooking appliances for use by residents shall be in 
compliance with all of the following: 
-

pages.text:
CHAPTER 4—SPECIAL DETAILED REQUIREMENTS BASED ON OCCUPANCY and 
use 
 
 
SBC 201-CC-2024 
359 
 
1 
The types of domestic cooking appliances 
shall be limited to ovens, cooktops, ranges, 
warmers, coffee makers and microwaves. 
2 
Domestic cooking appliances shall be limited 
to approved locations. 
3 
Cooktops and ranges shall be protected in 
accordance with Section 904.14 . 
4 
Cooktops and ranges shall be provided with 
a domestic cooking hood installed and 
constructed in accordance with Section 505 
of SBC 501. 
❖ 
See the commentaries to Sections 420.11  and 
420.11.2 . 
420.11.2  
Cooking appliances in sleeping 
rooms.  Cooktops, ranges and ovens shall not be 
installed or used in sleeping rooms. 
❖ 
This section prohibits ovens, cooktops and 
ranges from being used in sleeping rooms. 
This reflects the fact that cooktops and ranges 
are leading causes of fires in residential 
settings. This section does allow the use of 
other cooking appliances, such as microwaves 
and coffee makers, in sleeping rooms. 
However, individual colleges may have more 
restrictive rules that prohibit some of these 
appliances 
from 
being 
used 
in 
their 
dormitories. 
SECTION 421 HYDROGEN FUEL GAS 
ROOMS 
421.1  General. Where required by the  SBC 801, 
hydrogen fuel gas rooms shall be designed and 
constructed in accordance with Sections 421.1  
through 421.7 .  
❖ 
This section is simply stating that all hydrogen 
cutoff rooms are to be constructed in 
accordance with the provisions contained in 
Section 421. Hydrogen fuel gas rooms were 
created to address the increasing and 
emerging concepts of fuel cells that use 
hydrogen and sometimes generate hydrogen 
on-site to run the fuel cells. The provisions of 
this Section are coordinated with Section 5808 
of SBC 801. See also Section 406 and Section 
2309 of SBC 801 for requirements where 
hydrogen fuel is dispensed.   
421.2  Location. Hydrogen fuel gas rooms shall 
not be located below grade.  
❖ 
Restrictions against installation of hydrogen 
fuel gas rooms below grade are similar to those 
restricting the location of flammable and 
combustible liquids in basements. Explosion 
hazards are the 
primary concern, and 
placement of materials that have an ability to 
cause an explosion in below-grade spaces is 
not appropriate. Such spaces are more difficult 
to evacuate, create a fire explosion hazard to 
the structure above and are very difficult for the 
fire department to address.  
421.3  Design and construction. Hydrogen fuel 
gas rooms not classified as Group H shall be 
separated from other areas of the building in 
accordance with Section 509.1 .  
❖ 
Hydrogen fuel gas rooms are required to be 
separated by not less than 1-hour fire-
resistance-rated fire barriers and horizontal 
assemblies, which is consistent with the 
requirements in Table 509.1 for incidental 
uses. In addition, the classification of the space 
will 
affect 
the 
separation 
requirements. 
Hydrogen fuel gas rooms can contain any 
amount of hydrogen, but where the maximum 
allowable quantities are exceeded, the room 
can no longer be considered an incidental use 
and instead must be classified as a Group H-2 
occupancy. A Group H-2 must then be 
addressed as a separated occupancy. In all 
cases, separation in accordance with Table 
508.4 would apply in addition to the applicable 
occupancy-specific requirements.  
421.3.1  Pressure control. Hydrogen fuel gas 
rooms shall be provided with a ventilation system 
designed to maintain the room at a negative 
pressure in relation to surrounding rooms and 
spaces.  
❖ 
This section requires hydrogen fuel gas rooms 
to be maintained at a negative pressure with 
regard to surrounding spaces to provide some 
level of protection against a flammable mixture 
being attained in other parts of the building.  
421.3.2  Windows. Operable windows in interior 
walls shall not be permitted. Fixed windows shall 
be permitted where in accordance with Section 716.  
❖ 
Operable windows are prohibited to further 
reduce the likelihood of allowing hydrogen from 
escaping into the room and entering other 
portions of the building that may not be properly 
ventilated. 
An 
operable 
window 
could 
inadvertently be left in the open or partially 
open position and go unnoticed. Fixed window 
openings must meet the requirements of 
Section 716 to ensure that the requirements for 
opening protectives are met. More specifically, 
the proper fire protection ratings for openings 
in fire barriers are required.  
421.4  Exhaust ventilation. Hydrogen fuel gas 
rooms shall be provided with mechanical exhaust 
-

pages.text:
CHAPTER 4—SPECIAL DETAILED REQUIREMENTS BASED ON OCCUPANCY and 
use 
 
 
SBC 201-CC-2024 
360 
 
ventilation in accordance with the applicable 
provisions of Section 502.16.1 of SBC 501.  
❖ 
The purpose of this section is to prevent a 
dangerous accumulation of flammable gas in 
the room through the use of an exhaust 
ventilation 
system. 
The 
Sourcebook 
for 
Hydrogen 
Applications 
recommends 
ventilation at the rate of 5.08 liters per second 
per square meters of floor area, which is 
consistent with the requirements in Chapter 5 
of SBC 501.  
421.5  Gas detection system. Hydrogen fuel gas 
rooms shall be provided with a gas detection system 
that complies with Sections 421.5.1 , 421.5.2 , and 
916.  
❖ 
Some gases contain additives that produce 
pungent odors for easy recognition. Systems 
using non-odorized gases, such as hydrogen 
and liquid natural gas, must utilize gas 
detection systems to detect leaks. This section 
specifically requires such detection due to the 
hazards associated with a buildup of hydrogen 
at hazardous levels within a building.  
421.5.1  System design. Activation of a gas 
detection alarm shall result in both of the following: 
1 
Initiation of distinct audible and visible alarm 
signals both inside and outside of the 
hydrogen fuel gas room. 
2 
Automatic activation of the mechanical 
exhaust ventilation system. 
❖ 
The detection system must initiate the 
operations specified in Section 916 at any time 
that the flammable gas concentration exceeds 
one-fourth of the concentration necessary to 
support combustion. Early detection of the 
presence of a flammable gas will allow 
adequate mitigation procedures to be taken. 
Hydrogen fires are not normally extinguished 
until the supply of hydrogen has been shut off 
because of the danger of reignition or 
explosion. A gas detection system in the room 
or space housing a gaseous hydrogen system 
results in early notification of a leak that is 
occurring before the escaping gas reaches a 
hazardous concentration.  
421.5.2  Failure of the gas detection system. 
Failure of the gas detection system shall 
automatically activate the mechanical exhaust 
ventilation system, stop hydrogen generation, and 
cause a trouble signal to sound at an approved 
location.  
❖ 
Systems must be designed to be self-
monitoring and fail-safe in that all safety 
systems are activated to alert any occupants 
that a problem exists and to prevent more 
hydrogen from being generated by any 
appliances in the room where hazardous 
conditions cannot be monitored.  
421.6  Explosion control. Explosion control shall 
be provided where required by Section 414.5.1 .  
❖ 
The requirements of this Section are intended 
to address the circumstance resulting from a 
catastrophic failure of the cutoff room. These 
requirements are the final safeguard in case 
safety features such as interlocked doors, 
ventilation systems and gas detection systems 
should fail. An ignited hydrogen mixture 
produces large quantities of heat, causing a 
rapid expansion of the surrounding air. This 
can cause a pressure increase in a confined 
space and a catastrophic failure. Explosion 
control methods are identified in Section 911 of 
SBC 801 to prevent such a catastrophic failure. 
The 
explosion 
control 
requirements 
for 
hydrogen are consistent with the requirements 
in NFPA 55 and the Sourcebook for Hydrogen 
Applications.  
421.7  Standby power. Mechanical ventilation 
and gas detection systems shall be provided with a 
standby power system in accordance with Section 
2702.  
❖ 
The ventilation system and gas detection 
system are life safety systems and, therefore, 
must be dependable. Both safety systems 
must remain active in the event of a power 
failure of the primary power supply. Hydrogen 
is a colorless, odorless gas; a release might go 
undetected if detection systems are not 
functioning. The accumulation of hydrogen in 
an unventilated area can lead to mixtures in the 
flammable range if safety systems and 
mechanical ventilation systems are not in 
operation. Chapter 50 of SBC 801 addresses 
emergency and standby power requirements 
for emergency systems. It also allows an 
exception to the requirement for systems that 
are fail-safe (see SBC 801 Section 5004.7.2). 
This exception may be used in cutoff rooms 
where hydrogen is generated, but not stored. 
Any storage of hydrogen within the cutoff room 
would not qualify for the exception because, in 
the event of a power failure, there will be no 
way to detect or ventilate a release from a 
storage vessel.  
SECTION 422 AMBULATORY CARE 
FACILITIES 
422.1  General. 
Occupancies 
classified 
as 
ambulatory care facilities shall comply with the 
provisions of Sections 422.1 through 422.7 and 
other applicable provisions of this code.  
-

SECTION 422 AMBULATORY CARE 
FACILITIES 
422.1  General. 
Occupancies 
classified 
as 
ambulatory care facilities shall comply with the 
provisions of Sections 422.1 through 422.7 and 
other applicable provisions of this code.  
-

pages.text:
CHAPTER 4—SPECIAL DETAILED REQUIREMENTS BASED ON OCCUPANCY and 
use 
 
 
SBC 201-CC-2024 
361 
 
❖ 
Complex outpatient surgeries outside of the 
hospital are now commonplace. They are 
performed in facilities often called “day surgery 
centers” or “ambulatory surgical centers” 
because care recipients are able to walk in and 
walk out the same day. Procedures render care 
recipients 
temporarily 
incapable of self-
preservation by application of nerve blocks, 
sedation or anesthesia. Care recipients in 
these facilities typically recover quickly. A 
definition of “Ambulatory care facility” is 
provided in Section 202.  
 
The code identifies medical care Group I 
occupancies as having 24-hour stay. Without a 
24-hour stay, these surgery centers are 
classified as Group B. Strictly regulating such 
occupancies as a typical Group B occupancy is 
considered inappropriate, as this would allow 
the rendering of an unlimited number of people 
incapable of self-preservation with no more 
protection than a business office. These types 
of facilities contain distinctly different hazards 
to life and safety than other business 
occupancies, such as: 
1 
Care 
recipients 
incapable 
of 
self-
preservation 
require 
rescue 
by 
other 
occupants or fire personnel.  
2 
Medical staff must stabilize the patient prior 
to evacuation; therefore, staff may require 
evacuation as well.  
3 
Use of oxidizing medical gases, such as 
oxygen and nitrous oxide.  
4 
Prevalence of surgical fires.  
❖ 
These requirements provide a scaled approach 
to protection. The occupancy classification is a 
Group B, but with some enhanced safety 
features 
focused 
on 
the 
concern 
with 
occupants being incapable of self-preservation 
on 
a 
temporary 
basis. 
The 
enhanced 
requirements are based on the concepts in the 
regulation of the Group I-2 occupancy 
requirements found in Section 407. 
422.2  Separation. Ambulatory care facilities 
where the potential for four or more care recipients 
are to be incapable of self-preservation at any time,  
shall be separated from adjacent spaces, corridors 
or tenants with a fire partition installed in 
accordance with Section 708.  
❖ 
In a multi-tenant or mixed occupancy building 
where there are other uses present in addition 
to an ambulatory care facility, a degree of fire 
separation between the ambulatory care facility 
and those nonrelated spaces is required where 
the ambulatory care facility is intended to have 
at least four care recipients incapable of self-
preservation at any one time. The minimum 
separation, a fire partition complying with 
Section 708, is viewed as an important tool in 
isolating the ambulatory care portion of building 
from fire hazards that may occur in other 
portions of the building. Where a building is 
considered wholly as an ambulatory care 
facility, or where the ambulatory care facility 
occupies the entire story of a multistory 
building, there is no requirement for a fire 
partition to be constructed. (See separation 
example in Commentary Figure 422.2). 
422.3  Smoke 
compartments. 
Where 
the 
aggregate area of one or more ambulatory care 
facilities is greater than 900 m2 on one story, the 
story shall be provided with a smoke barrier to 
subdivide the story into not fewer than two smoke 
compartments. The area of any one such smoke 
compartment shall be not greater than 2000 m2. The 
distance of travel from any point in a smoke 
compartment to a smoke barrier door shall be not 
greater than 60 m. The smoke barrier shall be 
installed in accordance with Section 709 with the 
exception that smoke barriers shall be continuous 
from outside wall to an outside wall, a floor to a 
floor, or from a smoke barrier to a smoke barrier or 
a combination thereof.  
❖ 
In larger facilities, the creation of smoke 
compartments is required to allow a protect-in-
place environment. These compartments allow 
staff a safer environment to stabilize the care 
recipients before evacuation, and protection for 
fire personnel who may have to evacuate both 
care recipients and staff. The maximum size of 
a smoke compartment is limited to 2000 m2, 
consistent with the maximum size allowed for 
Group I-2, Condition 1 occupancies. It is 
important to note that each floor must be 
divided into at least two smoke compartments 
where the total floor area exceeds 900 m2 on 
each story. Conversely, smoke compartments 
are not required for ambulatory care facilities 
with a floor area that does not exceed 900 m2 
on each story. The 900 m2 threshold is based 
on a story-by-story basis and is based on the 
aggregate 
floor 
areas 
where 
multiple 
ambulatory care facilities are present. The 
travel distance of 60 m to a door providing 
egress from the smoke compartment is the 
same 
requirement 
as 
for 
Group 
I-2 
occupancies 
[see 
smoke 
compartment 
examples in Commentary Figures 422.3(1) and 
422.3(2)].   
422.3.1  Means of egress. Where ambulatory care 
facilities require smoke compartmentation in 
accordance with Section 422.3 , the fire safety 
evacuation plans provided in accordance with 
-

are not required for ambulatory care facilities 
with a floor area that does not exceed 900 m2 
on each story. The 900 m2 threshold is based 
on a story-by-story basis and is based on the 
aggregate 
floor 
areas 
where 
multiple 
ambulatory care facilities are present. The 
travel distance of 60 m to a door providing 
egress from the smoke compartment is the 
same 
requirement 
as 
for 
Group 
I-2 
occupancies 
[see 
smoke 
compartment 
examples in Commentary Figures 422.3(1) and 
422.3(2)].   
422.3.1  Means of egress. Where ambulatory care 
facilities require smoke compartmentation in 
accordance with Section 422.3 , the fire safety 
evacuation plans provided in accordance with

pages.text:
CHAPTER 4—SPECIAL DETAILED REQUIREMENTS BASED ON OCCUPANCY and 
use 
 
 
SBC 201-CC-2024 
362 
 
Section 1002.2 shall identify the building 
components necessary to support a defend-in-place 
emergency response in accordance with Sections 
404 and 408 of the  SBC 801.  
❖ 
Defend-in-place, or protect in place, is a 
concept that has long been employed as the 
preferred method of fire response in hospitals 
due to the fragile nature of the occupants. 
Occupants in this setting, and similarly in 
ambulatory care facilities, are often dependent 
on the building infrastructure given that 
immediate evacuation would place their lives at 
risk. This infrastructure typically includes life 
support systems such as medical gases, 
emergency power and environmental controls 
that rely on continued building operation. 
Previous versions of this code have created a 
tried and tested set of requirements to support 
this concept, such as smoke compartmentation 
and areas of refuge. However, previous codes 
have not specifically described the concept of 
occupants remaining within a building during a 
fire emergency, leading to confusion and 
misapplication during design and enforcement. 
The concept is predicated on the use of smoke 
compartmentation.  
422.3.2  Refuge area. Not less than 2.8 m2 for each 
non-ambulatory care recipient shall be provided 
within the aggregate area of corridors, care recipient 
rooms, treatment rooms, lounge or dining areas and 
other low-hazard areas within each smoke 
compartment. Each occupant of an ambulatory care 
facility shall be provided with access to a refuge 
area without passing through or utilizing adjacent 
tenant spaces.  
❖ 
This requirement is very similar to that found in 
Section 407.5.1 for Group I-2 occupancies. 
The purpose is to provide adequate space for 
care recipients, on beds, who might be 
relocated to an adjacent smoke compartment. 
Since the facility may still be in operation during 
the fire, space must be provided in corridors, 
patient rooms, treatment rooms, lounge or 
dining areas and other low-hazard areas for 
both the care recipients being relocated and 
the care recipients being treated in that smoke 
compartment. Section 407.5.1 acknowledges 
that there may be floors that do not house care 
recipients who are confined to beds or litters. It 
is also important that access to a refuge area 
be provided without requiring the occupant to 
travel through any other tenant space.  
422.3.3  Independent egress. A means of egress 
shall be provided from each smoke compartment 
created by smoke barriers without having to return 
through the smoke compartment from which means 
of egress originated.  
❖ 
This Section is conceptually linked to Section 
407.5.4 and requires that occupants should be 
able to exit the building without having to 
reenter a smoke compartment from where they 
started. Although the strategy is to protect in 
place, occupants should not have to travel 
back through a smoke compartment to 
ultimately exit the building. This prevents the 
creation of a dead-end smoke compartment 
[see Commentary Figures 407.5, 407.5.4(1) 
and 407.5.4(2)].  
422.4  Automatic sprinkler systems. Automatic 
sprinkler systems shall be provided for ambulatory 
care facilities in accordance with Section 903.2.2 .  
❖ 
Section 422.4 is part of the package of 
enhanced 
Group 
B 
requirements 
to 
accommodate the risk to occupants that is 
higher than the typical business occupancy but 
does not warrant classification as a Group I-2 
occupancy. The actual sprinkler requirements 
are located in Section 903.2.2. The specific 
requirements apply where there are four or 
more care recipients incapable of self-
preservation or where one or more care 
recipients are located on a story other than the 
level of exit discharge. The entire floor area 
meeting either one of the criteria must be 
sprinklered. 
Note 
also 
that 
where 
the 
ambulatory care facility is on other than the 
level of exit discharge, the floors below such 
floor containing the ambulatory care facility 
must 
also 
be 
provided 
with 
automatic 
sprinklers.  
422.5  Fire alarm systems. A fire alarm system 
shall be provided for ambulatory care facilities in 
accordance with Section 907.2.2 .  
❖ 
As with the sprinkler requirements, the alarm 
requirements are actually located in Chapter 9. 
More specifically, Section 907.2.2.1 requires a 
manual fire alarm system in any fire area 
containing a Group B ambulatory care facility. 
Further, an automatic smoke detection system 
is required within ambulatory care facility and 
public use spaces, such as lobbies and 
corridors, except where all areas of the building 
are protected by an automatic sprinkler 
system. Where the exception is applied, the 
sprinkler system is required to activate the 
occupant notification appliances upon sprinkler 
water 
flow 
(see 
commentary, 
Section 
907.2.2.1). The scope of the smoke detection 
was purposely narrowed to limit the amount of 
retrofit measures that a building owner would 
be required to undertake where a new 
-
pages.code_text:
CHAPTER 4—SPECIAL DETAILED REQUIREMENTS BASED ON OCCUPANCY and 
use 
 
 
SBC 201-CC-2024 
362 
 
Section 1002.2 shall identify the building 
components necessary to support a defend-in-place 
emergency response in accordance with Sections 
404 and 408 of the  SBC 801.
pages.commentary:
Defend-in-place, or protect in place, is a 
concept that has long been employed as the 
preferred method of fire response in hospitals 
due to the fragile nature of the occupants. 
Occupants in this setting, and similarly in 
ambulatory care facilities, are often dependent 
on the building infrastructure given that 
immediate evacuation would place their lives at 
risk. This infrastructure typically includes life 
support systems such as medical gases, 
emergency power and environmental controls 
that rely on continued building operation. 
Previous versions of this code have created a 
tried and tested set of requirements to support 
this concept, such as smoke compartmentation 
and areas of refuge. However, previous codes 
have not specifically described the concept of 
occupants remaining within a building during a 
fire emergency, leading to confusion and 
misapplication during design and enforcement. 
The concept is predicated on the use of smoke 
compartmentation.  
422.3.2  Refuge area. Not less than 2.8 m2 for each 
non-ambulatory care recipient shall be provided 
within the aggregate area of corridors, care recipient 
rooms, treatment rooms, lounge or dining areas and 
other low-hazard areas within each smoke 
compartment. Each occupant of an ambulatory care 
facility shall be provided with access to a refuge 
area without passing through or utilizing adjacent 
tenant spaces.

❖ This requirement is very similar to that found in 
Section 407.5.1 for Group I-2 occupancies. 
The purpose is to provide adequate space for 
care recipients, on beds, who might be 
relocated to an adjacent smoke compartment. 
Since the facility may still be in operation during 
the fire, space must be provided in corridors, 
patient rooms, treatment rooms, lounge or 
dining areas and other low-hazard areas for 
both the care recipients being relocated and 
the care recipients being treated in that smoke 
compartment. Section 407.5.1 acknowledges 
that there may be floors that do not house care 
recipients who are confined to beds or litters. It 
is also important that access to a refuge area 
be provided without requiring the occupant to 
travel through any other tenant space.  
422.3.3  Independent egress. A means of egress 
shall be provided from each smoke compartment 
created by smoke barriers without having to return 
through the smoke compartment from which means 
of egress originated.

❖ This Section is conceptually linked to Section 
407.5.4 and requires that occupants should be 
able to exit the building without having to 
reenter a smoke compartment from where they 
started. Although the strategy is to protect in 
place, occupants should not have to travel 
back through a smoke compartment to 
ultimately exit the building. This prevents the 
creation of a dead-end smoke compartment 
[see Commentary Figures 407.5, 407.5.4(1) 
and 407.5.4(2)].  
422.4  Automatic sprinkler systems. Automatic 
sprinkler systems shall be provided for ambulatory 
care facilities in accordance with Section 903.2.2 .

❖ Section 422.4 is part of the package of 
enhanced 
Group 
B 
requirements 
to 
accommodate the risk to occupants that is 
higher than the typical business occupancy but 
does not warrant classification as a Group I-2 
occupancy. The actual sprinkler requirements 
are located in Section 903.2.2. The specific 
requirements apply where there are four or 
more care recipients incapable of self-
preservation or where one or more care 
recipients are located on a story other than the 
level of exit discharge. The entire floor area

Note 
also 
that 
where 
the 
ambulatory care facility is on other than the 
level of exit discharge, the floors below such 
floor containing the ambulatory care facility 
must 
also 
be 
provided 
with 
automatic 
sprinklers.  
422.5  Fire alarm systems. A fire alarm system 
shall be provided for ambulatory care facilities in 
accordance with Section 907.2.2 .

❖ As with the sprinkler requirements, the alarm

pages.text:
CHAPTER 4—SPECIAL DETAILED REQUIREMENTS BASED ON OCCUPANCY and 
use 
 
 
SBC 201-CC-2024 
363 
 
ambulatory care tenant was established in an 
existing building.  
422.6  Electrical systems.  In ambulatory care 
facilities, the essential electrical system for 
electrical components, equipment and systems shall 
be designed and constructed in accordance with the 
provisions of Chapter 27 and NFPA 99. 
❖ 
NFPA 99: Health Care Facilities Code provides 
an assessment tool for determining what 
constitutes essential electrical systems. It 
provides a risk-based approach to determine 
the need for an essential electrical system, 
what class system is required and general 
design requirements for each type of system. 
422.7  Domestic cooking. Installation of cooking 
appliances used in domestic cooking facilities shall 
comply with all of the following: 
1 
The types of cooking appliances permitted 
are limited to ovens, cooktops, ranges, 
warmers and microwaves. 
2 
Domestic cooking hoods installed and 
constructed in accordance with Section 505 
of SBC 501 shall be provided over cooktops 
or ranges. 
3 
A shutoff for the fuel and electrical supply to 
the cooking equipment shall be provided in a 
location to which only staff has access. 
4 
A timer shall be provided that automatically 
deactivates the cooking appliances within a 
period of not more than 120 minutes. 
5 
A portable fire extinguisher shall be 
provided. Installation shall be in accordance 
with Section 906 and the extinguisher shall 
be located within a 9 m distance of travel 
from each domestic cooking appliance. 
❖ 
In an ambulatory care facility, kitchens may be 
provided as part of nutrition counseling or care 
recipient training/rehabilitation facilities. See 
also the commentary to Section 407.2.6 . 
 
The appliances that are permitted to be in a 
domestic cooking facility are electric or gas 
ranges, cooktops, stoves, ovens, warmers and 
microwaves. Toasters and toaster ovens fall 
into the warmer or oven category. Deep-fat 
fryers, charcoal grills, wood-burning ovens or 
commercial 
cooking 
appliances 
are 
not 
permitted in domestic cooking facilities. Only 
the cooktops and ranges are required to have a 
domestic cooking hood. 
 
The cooktop or range must have a shutoff 
switch that deactivates the appliance and is 
accessible only to staff. This may be a switch 
inside a locked cabinet, a keyed switch or an 
electronic switch with pass code or similar 
security. It may be located on the wall near the 
appliance, in a kitchen cabinet or inside the 
pantry or other room. The intent is that the 
cooktop or range is only able to be used when 
staff are aware that it is being used or if staff is 
able to supervise. Similarly, a timer is required 
to deactivate the cooktop, range or oven after 
120 minutes in case a staff member was 
distracted by, or tending to, an urgent care 
situation. The timer should be able to be reset 
by the staff member, as needed, when cooking 
something that requires a cooking time of 
longer than 120 minutes. 
 
A portable fire extinguisher must be located 
within 9 m of each cooking appliance. The 
intent is that a staff member can quickly reach 
it in the event of an emergency. Ideally, it would 
be located in the same room as the cooking 
appliance but can be located outside the room 
as long as the travel distance does not exceed 
9 m. It is best practice that this a Class K fire 
extinguisher. 
SECTION 423 STORM SHELTERS 
423.1  General. This Section applies to the 
construction of storm shelters constructed as 
separate detached buildings or constructed as rooms 
or spaces within buildings for the purpose of 
providing protection from storms that produce high 
winds, such as tornadoes and hurricanes, during the 
storm. This Section specifies where storm shelters 
are required and provides requirements for the 
design and construction of storm shelters. Design of 
facilities for use as emergency shelters after the 
storm are outside the scope of ICC 500 and shall 
comply with Table 1604.5 as a Risk Category IV 
Structure. 
❖ 
Post-tornado investigations have determined 
that general SBC 201-compliant facilities are 
susceptible to significant building damage 
(both structural and cladding) if struck by strong 
or violent tornadoes. While the damage may 
not result in a total structural failure, it can 
cause loss of life and injuries. The purpose of 
the storm shelter is to provide life-safety 
protection for people during tornadoes or 
hurricanes. 
 
The standard does not address the use of the 
shelter as a post-storm recovery facility. Storms 
shelters intended to also serve as post-storm 
recovery facilities must be designed as Risk 
Category IV structures in accordance with 
Table 1604.5, where risk categories are 
assigned 
to 
buildings 
to 
account 
for 
consequences of building failure. Buildings 
-

SECTION 423 STORM SHELTERS 
423.1  General. This Section applies to the 
construction of storm shelters constructed as 
separate detached buildings or constructed as rooms 
or spaces within buildings for the purpose of 
providing protection from storms that produce high 
winds, such as tornadoes and hurricanes, during the 
storm. This Section specifies where storm shelters 
are required and provides requirements for the 
design and construction of storm shelters. Design of 
facilities for use as emergency shelters after the 
storm are outside the scope of ICC 500 and shall 
comply with Table 1604.5 as a Risk Category IV 
Structure.

❖ Post-tornado investigations have determined 
that general SBC 201-compliant facilities are 
susceptible to significant building damage 
(both structural and cladding) if struck by strong 
or violent tornadoes. While the damage may 
not result in a total structural failure, it can 
cause loss of life and injuries. The purpose of 
the storm shelter is to provide life-safety 
protection for people during tornadoes or 
hurricanes. 
 
The standard does not address the use of the 
shelter as a post-storm recovery facility. Storms 
shelters intended to also serve as post-storm 
recovery facilities must be designed as Risk 
Category IV structures in accordance with 
Table 1604.5, where risk categories are 
assigned 
to 
buildings 
to 
account 
for 
consequences of building failure. Buildings 
-

pages.text:
CHAPTER 4—SPECIAL DETAILED REQUIREMENTS BASED ON OCCUPANCY and 
use 
 
 
SBC 201-CC-2024 
364 
 
assigned higher risk categories are required to 
meet higher multihazard design criteria that 
result in greater overall resiliency. In the case of 
essential facilities such as post-storm recovery 
shelters, the Risk Category IV designation is 
intended to preserve essential community 
services, so those personnel are available after 
an event to assist with recovery efforts. 
 
ICC 500-compliant storm shelters are only 
designed to provide protection during an 
extreme wind incident. Most are not sized or 
equipped for longer occupant duration needed 
for post-storm recovery facilities. ICC 500 
requirements for storm shelter resistance to 
wind pressures and windborne debris far 
exceed the wind design criteria required for 
Risk Category IV structures. Because storm 
shelters are often located within host buildings, 
the exception in Section 1604.5.1 allows the 
host building to maintain the risk category 
designation independent of the storm shelter 
unless the storm shelter is also designated to 
serve as poststorm recovery facility. The 
exception is important because additional cost 
required to upgrade the storm shelter host 
building to comply with the potentially higher 
Risk Category IV requirements would ultimately 
serve to discourage storm shelter construction. 
 
Sections 423.4 and 423.5 specify two instances 
where storm shelters are required in buildings 
constructed in compliance with the code.   
423.2  Construction. Storm shelters shall be 
constructed in accordance with this code and ICC 
500 and shall be designated as hurricane shelters, 
tornado shelters, or combined hurricane and 
tornado shelters. Buildings or structures that are 
also designated as emergency shelters shall also 
comply with Table 1604.5 as Risk Category IV 
structures. 
Any storm shelter not required by this Section shall 
be permitted to be constructed, provided that such 
structures meet the requirements of this code and 
ICC 500. 
❖ 
ICC 500 provides requirements for the design 
and construction of shelters to protect people 
from the violent winds of hurricanes and 
tornadoes. The standard includes special 
requirements for structural design, including 
wind loads that are considerably higher than 
the wind loads required by Chapter 16 for all 
structures. Storm shelters are commonly also 
used for other purposes during normal building 
operation. Examples include gymnasiums, 
classrooms and hallways in school buildings, 
and locker rooms and bathrooms in a fire 
station. Buildings used for the sole purpose as 
storm shelters are common at manufactured 
home parks. 
 
Residential storm shelters are defined as 
shelters for dwelling units and as shelters with 
an occupant load of 16 or fewer. Any storm 
shelters not meeting that definition would be 
considered 
community 
storm 
shelters, 
including shelter for larger groups of homes and 
apartment 
buildings, 
as 
well 
as 
other 
occupancies. 
 
Wind loads for tornado and hurricane storm 
shelters are based on wind speed maps 
developed specifically for ICC 500. The wind 
speeds are greater than those shown in wind 
speed maps in Chapter 16. 
 
The high wind speeds in tornadoes and 
hurricanes produce windborne debris, turning 
construction materials into deadly missiles. The 
standard contains missile criteria, impact test 
methods and pass-fail criteria from windborne 
debris 
penetration. 
The 
standard 
also 
addresses impact loads on the shelter roof due 
to lay-down hazards and falling debris hazards. 
Additional storm-related hazards that shelters 
must resist per ICC 500 include extreme 
rainfall, 
inland 
flooding 
and 
storm-surge 
flooding. 
 
ICC 500 addresses nonstructural issues, as 
well. Critical support systems are required to 
remain functional for 24 hours for hurricane 
storm shelters and 2 hours for tornado storm 
shelters. The standard addresses minimum 
requirements for ventilation air, sanitation 
facilities, water supply, lighting and other 
minimal power needs. 
 
It should be noted that the entrances and exits 
to community storm shelters are required to be 
accessible in accordance with ICC/American 
National 
Standards 
Institute 
A117.1, 
Accessible 
and 
Useable 
Buildings 
and 
Facilities. In addition, the occupant load 
requirements are such that minimum usable 
floor area will need to account for wheelchair 
space. 
423.3  Occupancy classification. The occupancy 
classification for a storm shelter shall be determined 
in accordance with this Section. 
❖ 
Occupancy classifications for storm shelters 
were adopted from ICC 500 to provide direct 
access to all code users. The correlated 
provisions are intended to facilitate early 
planning for: storm shelter projects or projects 
where 
storm 
shelters 
are 
required 
in 
accordance with Sections 423.4 and 423.5 , or 
where storm shelters are being considered for 
inclusion within a host building. 
-

pages.text:
CHAPTER 4—SPECIAL DETAILED REQUIREMENTS BASED ON OCCUPANCY and 
use 
 
 
SBC 201-CC-2024 
365 
 
423.3.1  Dedicated storm shelters. A facility 
designed to be occupied solely as a storm shelter 
shall be classified as Group A-3 for the 
determination of requirements other than those 
covered in ICC 500. 
Exceptions: 
1 
The occupancy category for dedicated storm 
shelters with an occupant load of fewer than 
50 persons as determined in accordance with 
ICC 500 shall be in accordance with Section 
303. 
2 
The occupancy category for a dedicated 
residential storm shelter shall be the Group R 
occupancy served. 
❖ 
Where a facility is provided for the sole purpose 
of functioning as a storm shelter, it was 
determined that the facility would most likely 
resemble a Group A-3 facility as described in 
SBC 201. 
 
Exception 1 recognizes that a relaxation of wall 
and ceiling finish requirements in corridors, 
number of plumbing fixtures in restrooms and 
bathrooms, 
and 
slope 
and 
handrail 
requirements for means of egress may not be 
appropriate for a small shelter serving at most 
a few dozen people. The exception points to a 
provision in SBC 201 that allows a building or 
structure used for assembly purposes but 
having an occupant load less than 50 persons 
to be designated as a Group B occupancy 
rather than a Group A occupancy. 
 
Exception 2 addresses congregate living 
facilities, lodging houses and custodial care 
facilities housed in buildings with one or two 
dwelling units and 16 or fewer occupants 
classified as Group R-3 or R-4 under SBC 201. 
It is intended to prevent any possible issues that 
might stem from having a Group B (as 
permitted by Exception 1) structure adjacent or 
accessory to a Group R building. 
423.3.2  Storm shelters within host buildings. 
Where designated storm shelters are constructed as 
a room or space within a host building that will 
normally be occupied for other purposes, the 
requirements of this code for the occupancy of the 
building, or the individual rooms or spaces thereof, 
shall apply unless otherwise required by ICC 500. 
❖ 
Frequently, storm shelters are designated 
rooms or spaces within larger facilities (or “host 
buildings”) that are designed to function as 
storm shelters in tornadoes or hurricanes but 
are used for other purposes under normal 
circumstances. A common example is a 
classroom, study hall, gymnasium or cafeteria 
space in a school building. The purpose of 
adding this statement to the standard is to 
establish that the standard is not intended to 
supplant the requirements for the normal use 
of the space. For instance, determination of 
occupant load for the use of the space as a 
shelter does not change the occupant load of 
the space in its “normal” function. 
423.4  Critical emergency operations. In areas 
where the shelter design wind speed for tornados in 
accordance with Figure 304.2(1) of ICC 500 is 112 
m/s, 911 call stations, emergency operation centers 
and fire, rescue, ambulance and police stations shall 
comply with Table 1604.5 as a Risk Category IV 
structure and shall be provided with a storm shelter 
constructed in accordance with ICC 500.  
❖ 
Critical 
emergency 
operations 
facilities—
specifically 911 call stations; emergency 
operation centers; and fire, rescue, ambulance, 
and police stations centers— are essential for 
the delivery of vital services or protection of a 
community. Section 424.4 requires ICC 500-
compliant storm shelters in critical emergency 
operations facilities constructed in accordance 
with this code and located in areas with 
greatest risk of strong tornadoes. Unlike the 
Group E occupancies addressed in Section 
423.5 , critical emergency operations facilities 
have 
no 
minimum 
occupancy 
for 
the 
application of Section 423.4 . 
 
The geographical limitation of Sections 423.4  
and 423.5  are critical to applying the provisions 
of this code. That is, the only jurisdictions 
affected by Sections 423.4 and 423.5 are areas 
where the shelter design wind speed for 
tornadoes is 112 m/s as defined in Figure 
304.2(1) of ICC 500. Figure 304.2(1) of ICC 
500. The types of facilities that are required to 
have shelters are locations that house 
emergency responders or systems that a 
community would want operational after a 
tornado event. 
 
An option to providing a storm shelter within 
these buildings is to construct the entire 
building as a storm shelter. The advantage of 
constructing the whole building as a shelter is 
that, should a tornado hit the building, the 
building has a better chance of surviving the 
storm and therefore being ready to provide 
emergency 
services 
to 
the 
community 
immediately after the storm passes. 
423.5  Group E occupancies. In areas where the 
shelter design wind speed for tornados is 110 m/s, 
all Group E occupancies with an aggregate 
occupant load of 50 or more shall have a storm 
shelter constructed in accordance with ICC 500.

pages.code_text:
CHAPTER 4—SPECIAL DETAILED REQUIREMENTS BASED ON OCCUPANCY and 
use 
 
 
SBC 201-CC-2024 
365 
 
423.3.1  Dedicated storm shelters. A facility 
designed to be occupied solely as a storm shelter 
shall be classified as Group A-3 for the 
determination of requirements other than those 
covered in ICC 500. 
Exceptions: 
1 
The occupancy category for dedicated storm 
shelters with an occupant load of fewer than 
50 persons as determined in accordance with 
ICC 500 shall be in accordance with Section 
303. 
2 
The occupancy category for a dedicated 
residential storm shelter shall be the Group R 
occupancy served.
pages.commentary:
Where a facility is provided for the sole purpose 
of functioning as a storm shelter, it was 
determined that the facility would most likely 
resemble a Group A-3 facility as described in 
SBC 201. 
 
Exception 1 recognizes that a relaxation of wall 
and ceiling finish requirements in corridors, 
number of plumbing fixtures in restrooms and 
bathrooms, 
and 
slope 
and 
handrail 
requirements for means of egress may not be 
appropriate for a small shelter serving at most 
a few dozen people. The exception points to a 
provision in SBC 201 that allows a building or 
structure used for assembly purposes but 
having an occupant load less than 50 persons 
to be designated as a Group B occupancy 
rather than a Group A occupancy. 
 
Exception 2 addresses congregate living 
facilities, lodging houses and custodial care 
facilities housed in buildings with one or two 
dwelling units and 16 or fewer occupants 
classified as Group R-3 or R-4 under SBC 201. 
It is intended to prevent any possible issues that 
might stem from having a Group B (as 
permitted by Exception 1) structure adjacent or 
accessory to a Group R building. 
423.3.2  Storm shelters within host buildings. 
Where designated storm shelters are constructed as 
a room or space within a host building that will 
normally be occupied for other purposes, the 
requirements of this code for the occupancy of the 
building, or the individual rooms or spaces thereof, 
shall apply unless otherwise required by ICC 500.

❖ Frequently, storm shelters are designated 
rooms or spaces within larger facilities (or “host 
buildings”) that are designed to function as 
storm shelters in tornadoes or hurricanes but 
are used for other purposes under normal 
circumstances. A common example is a 
classroom, study hall, gymnasium or cafeteria 
space in a school building. The purpose of 
adding this statement to the standard is to 
establish that the standard is not intended to 
supplant the requirements for the normal use 
of the space. For instance, determination of 
occupant load for the use of the space as a 
shelter does not change the occupant load of 
the space in its “normal” function. 
423.4  Critical emergency operations. In areas 
where the shelter design wind speed for tornados in 
accordance with Figure 304.2(1) of ICC 500 is 112 
m/s, 911 call stations, emergency operation centers 
and fire, rescue, ambulance and police stations shall 
comply with Table 1604.5 as a Risk Category IV 
structure and shall be provided with a storm shelter 
constructed in accordance with ICC 500.

❖ Critical 
emergency 
operations 
facilities—
specifically 911 call stations; emergency 
operation centers; and fire, rescue, ambulance, 
and police stations centers— are essential for 
the delivery of vital services or protection of a 
community. Section 424.4 requires ICC 500-
compliant storm shelters in critical emergency 
operations facilities constructed in accordance 
with this code and located in areas with 
greatest risk of strong tornadoes. Unlike the 
Group E occupancies addressed in Section 
423.5 , critical emergency operations facilities 
have 
no 
minimum 
occupancy 
for 
the 
application of Section 423.4 . 
 
The geographical limitation of Sections 423.4  
and 423.5  are critical to applying the provisions 
of this code. That is, the only jurisdictions 
affected by Sections 423.4 and 423.5 are areas 
where the shelter design wind speed for 
tornadoes is 112 m/s as defined in Figure 
304.2(1) of ICC 500. Figure 304.2(1) of ICC 
500. The types of facilities that are required to 
have shelters are locations that house 
emergency responders or systems that a 
community would want operational after a 
tornado event. 
 
An option to providing a storm shelter within 
these buildings is to construct the entire 
building as a storm shelter. The advantage of 
constructing the whole building as a shelter is 
that, should a tornado hit the building, the 
building has a better chance of surviving the 
storm and therefore being ready to provide 
emergency 
services 
to 
the 
community 
immediately after the storm passes. 
423.5  Group E occupancies. In areas where the 
shelter design wind speed for tornados is 110 m/s, 
all Group E occupancies with an aggregate 
occupant load of 50 or more shall have a storm 
shelter constructed in accordance with ICC 500.

pages.text:
CHAPTER 4—SPECIAL DETAILED REQUIREMENTS BASED ON OCCUPANCY and 
use 
 
 
SBC 201-CC-2024 
366 
 
Exceptions: 
1 
Group E Day care facilities.  
2 
Group E occupancies accessory to mosques.  
3 
Buildings meeting the requirements for 
shelter design in ICC 500.  
❖ 
Section 423.5 requires ICC 500-compliant 
storm shelters in Group E Occupancies 
constructed in accordance with this code and 
with aggregate occupancy loads of 50 or more. 
As with Section 423.4 , the provisions of 
Section 423.5  apply only to buildings that are 
located in areas with greatest risk of strong 
tornadoes. The only exceptions to the storm 
shelter provisions of Section 423.5 are Group 
E Day care facilities (see Sections 305.2 and 
308.6.1), Group E occupancies accessory (i.e., 
classrooms; see Section 305.2.1 ), and 
buildings that otherwise meet the ICC 500 
requirements for shelter design. 
 
Tornado warning times are unpredictable and 
often very short (the average lead time for 
tornado warnings is 13 minutes). It is imperative 
that K-12 school students be able to shelter at 
their schools to have life-safety protection from 
a tornado in a storm shelter. Section 423.5.1  
specifies the size requirements of the storm 
shelter. There is nothing in the code that 
dictates that the capacity be provided in a single 
shelter. Having more than one shelter serve the 
needs of a building, or a complex of buildings, 
complies with the code. Some schools use the 
toilet rooms throughout the school as shelters 
to lessen the time and travel for students to 
access those shelters. In addition, the option 
exists to build the whole school as a shelter 
(Exception 
3). Schools 
that 
include 
an 
auditorium, gymnasium, or common space 
where large numbers are able to gather could 
use these spaces as high-capacity storm 
shelters. 
423.5.1  Required occupant capacity.  The 
required occupant capacity of the storm shelter shall 
include all of the buildings on the site and shall be 
the greater of the following: 
1 
The total occupant load of the classrooms, 
vocational rooms and offices in the Group E 
occupancy. 
2 
The occupant load of the largest indoor 
assembly space that is associated with the 
Group E occupancy. 
Exceptions: 
1 
Where a new building is being added on an 
existing Group E site, and where the new 
building is not of sufficient size to 
accommodate the required occupant capacity 
of the storm shelter for all of the buildings on 
the site, the storm shelter shall at a minimum 
accommodate the required occupant capacity 
for the new building. 
2 
Where approved by the building official, the 
required occupant capacity of the shelter 
shall be permitted to be reduced by the 
occupant capacity of any existing storm 
shelters on the site. 
❖ 
With the many uses of a school building, or a 
complex of buildings, not all spaces will be fully 
occupied at the same time. Worst-case 
occupant load is used for fire exiting where it is 
assumed 
all 
spaces 
are 
occupied 
simultaneously. But fire exiting total occupant 
load for the building is excessive for storm 
shelter design. The determination for the 
required capacity of the shelter is based on the 
number of staff and students that will be in the 
school during a typical school day or the largest 
indoor assembly space that would be fully 
utilized either during or outside of school hours, 
whichever is greater. If there are multiple 
indoor assembly spaces such as gymnasium, 
auditorium, cafeteria and library, the space with 
the largest occupant load is considered for 
determining capacity of the shelter. Thus, 
rather than the total occupant load of the 
building, the capacity of the shelter is 
appropriately 
based 
on 
occupant 
load 
described in the two scenarios described in 
Items 1 and 2. These provisions are not 
intended to require outdoor areas on the site 
(e.g., sports fields and bleachers) to be 
considered, since 
those areas are not 
buildings. 
 
If a new building is added to the site of an 
existing school, the code requires a storm 
shelter be included. The new building could be 
a separate structure on the site, or it could be 
an addition to an existing building that is 
separated from the existing building by a fire 
wall. A fire wall creates a separate building. If 
an addition is made but is not separated by a 
fire wall, the provisions of SBC 901 apply. Refer 
to ICC 500, Chapter 6 for fire safety 
requirements for storm shelters. 
 
If a new building is added to an existing Group 
E site, this is an opportunity to provide a storm 
shelter for that school. The designer would be 
responsible for determining the required storm 
shelter capacity for both the new building and 
for the rest of the facility on the site. If the new 
building is not large enough to house a shelter 
for the required occupant capacity of the entire 
-

determining capacity of the shelter. Thus, 
rather than the total occupant load of the 
building, the capacity of the shelter is 
appropriately 
based 
on 
occupant 
load 
described in the two scenarios described in 
Items 1 and 2. These provisions are not 
intended to require outdoor areas on the site 
(e.g., sports fields and bleachers) to be 
considered, since 
those areas are not 
buildings. 
 
If a new building is added to the site of an 
existing school, the code requires a storm 
shelter be included. The new building could be 
a separate structure on the site, or it could be 
an addition to an existing building that is 
separated from the existing building by a fire 
wall. A fire wall creates a separate building. If 
an addition is made but is not separated by a 
fire wall, the provisions of SBC 901 apply. Refer 
to ICC 500, Chapter 6 for fire safety 
requirements for storm shelters. 
 
If a new building is added to an existing Group 
E site, this is an opportunity to provide a storm 
shelter for that school. The designer would be 
responsible for determining the required storm 
shelter capacity for both the new building and 
for the rest of the facility on the site. If the new 
building is not large enough to house a shelter 
for the required occupant capacity of the entire 
-

pages.text:
CHAPTER 4—SPECIAL DETAILED REQUIREMENTS BASED ON OCCUPANCY and 
use 
 
 
SBC 201-CC-2024 
367 
 
Group E site, the shelter is required to at least 
accommodate the students and staff within that 
new building. If the new building is large enough 
to include a shelter that could accommodate all 
the students and staff on the site, the shelter is 
required to do so. If there is an existing storm 
shelter on the site, that can be considered to 
reduce the capacity required for the new 
shelter. The code is not intended to require the 
new building be increased in size just to meet 
the shelter provisions. 
423.5.2  Location.  Storm shelters shall be located 
within the buildings they serve or shall be located 
where the maximum distance of travel from not 
fewer than one exterior door of each building to a 
door of the shelter serving that building does not 
exceed 300 m. 
❖ 
This section requires the shelter to be within a 
building or within a distance of travel of 300 m 
or less. Where the shelter is remote, this would 
be approximately a 4-minute walk at 1.3 m/s, 
which is an average speed that humans tend to 
walk. Added to the estimated walk time is an 
assumed few hundred meters travel distance 
to first reach the exit of the building being 
served. Combined, the total travel time is 5 
minutes. This is consistent with current FEMA 
guidance for a maximum 5-minute walk time to 
reach the storm shelter. 
SECTION 424 PLAY STRUCTURES 
424.1  Play structures. Play structures installed 
inside all occupancies covered by this code that 
exceed 3 m in height or 14 m2 in area shall comply 
with Sections 424.2  through 424.5 .  
❖ 
Play structures have been regulated for some 
time by the code where such structures are 
located within covered mall buildings. The 
primary 
concern, 
consistent 
with 
other 
structures located within a covered mall 
building, is the combustibility of such play 
structures. Due to the potential fire hazards, 
the regulations are now applicable where such 
structures are located within any building 
regulated 
by 
this 
code, 
regardless 
of 
occupancy 
classification. 
The 
current 
provisions are essentially identical to those that 
historically regulated children's play structures 
located within covered mall buildings. Where 
these structures exceed 3 m in height or 14 m2 
in area, the provisions of Sections 424.2 
through 424.5 must be followed.  
424.2  Materials. Children’s play structures shall 
be constructed of noncombustible materials or of 
combustible materials that comply with the 
following: 
1 
Fire-retardant-treated wood complying with 
Section 2303.2 .  
2 
Light-transmitting plastics complying with 
Section 2606.  
3 
Foam plastics (including the pipe foam used 
in soft contained play equipment structures) 
having a maximum heat-release rate not 
greater than 100 kilowatts when tested in 
accordance with UL 1975 or when tested in 
accordance with NFPA 289, using the 20-kW 
ignition source.  
4 
Aluminium composite material (ACM) 
meeting the requirements of Class A interior 
finish in accordance with Chapter 8 when 
tested as an assembly in the maximum 
thickness intended for use.  
5 
Textiles and films complying with the fire 
propagation performance criteria contained 
in Test Method 1 or Test Method 2, as 
appropriate, of NFPA 701.  
6 
Plastic materials used to construct rigid 
components 
of 
soft-contained 
play 
equipment 
structures 
(such 
as 
tubes, 
windows, panels, junction boxes, pipes, 
slides and decks) exhibiting a peak rate of 
heat release not exceeding 400 kW/ m2 when 
tested in accordance with ASTM E1354 at an 
incident heat flux of 50 kW/m2 in the 
horizontal orientation at a thickness of 6 mm.  
7 
Ball pool balls, used in soft-contained play 
equipment structures, having a maximum 
heat-release rate not greater than 100 
kilowatts when tested in accordance with UL 
1975 or when tested in accordance with 
NFPA 289, using the 20-kW ignition source. 
The minimum specimen test size shall be 900 
mm by 900 mm by an average of 500 mm 
deep, and the balls shall be held in a box 
constructed of galvanized steel poultry 
netting wire mesh.  
8 
Foam plastics shall be covered by a fabric, 
coating or film meeting the fire propagation 
performance criteria contained in Test 
Method 1 or Test Method 2, as appropriate, 
of NFPA 701.  
9 
The floor covering placed under the 
children’s play structure shall exhibit a Class 
I interior floor finish classification, as 
described in Section 804, when tested in 
accordance with ASTM E648 or NFPA 253.  
-

regulated 
by

code,

of 
occupancy 
classification.

current 
provisions are essentially identical to those that

located within covered mall buildings. Where 
these structures exceed 3 m in height or 14 m2 
in area, the provisions of Sections 424.2 
through 424.5 must be followed.

components

structures

as

pages.text:
CHAPTER 4—SPECIAL DETAILED REQUIREMENTS BASED ON OCCUPANCY and 
use 
 
 
SBC 201-CC-2024 
368 
 
10 Interior finishes for structures exceeding 56 
m2 in area or 3 m in height shall have a flame 
spread index not greater than that specified in 
Table 803.13 for the occupancy group and 
location designated. Interior wall and ceiling 
finish materials tested in accordance with 
NFPA 286 and meeting the acceptance 
criteria of Section 803.1.1.1 , shall be 
permitted to be used where a Class A 
classification in accordance with ASTM E84 
or UL 723 is required. 
❖ 
The 
amount 
of 
combustible 
materials 
associated with play structures is rigidly limited. 
The provisions of Section 424.2 provide 10 
limitations for combustible materials that can 
be used to construct play equipment. These 
limitations are consistent with those previously 
applicable only to play structures located in 
covered mall buildings. The materials where 
tested in accordance with NFPA 701 must 
comply with the performance criteria of one of 
the two identified test methods. The historic 
“small-scale test” is no longer accepted.  
424.3  Fire protection. Play structures shall be 
provided with the same level of approved fire 
suppression and detection devices required for other 
structures in the same occupancy.  
❖ 
Play structures within a building must be 
provided with fire suppression and detection 
devices that provide an equal or better degree 
of fire protection than that required for the 
occupancy in which the play structures are 
located.  
424.4  Separation. Play structures shall have a 
horizontal 
separation 
from 
building 
walls, 
partitions and from elements of the means of egress 
of not less than 1.5 m. Playground structures shall 
have a horizontal separation from other play 
structures of not less than 6 m.  
❖ 
Multiple play structures must be isolated from 
each other by a minimum distance of 6 m. This 
separation reduces the concentration of 
combustible materials within a single area. A 
minimum separation of 1.5 m is also required 
between any play structure and portions of the 
building’s construction, including means of 
egress elements, to allow for emergency 
personnel access and to reduce fire spread 
potential.  
424.5  Area limits. Play structures shall be not 
greater than 56 m2 in area, unless a special 
investigation, acceptable to the building official, 
has demonstrated adequate fire safety.  
❖ 
Play structures are limited to a 56 m2 area 
limitation unless adequate fire safety is proven 
to the building official by way of a special 
investigation.  
424.5.1  Design. Play structures exceeding 55 m2 in 
area or 3 m in height shall be designed in 
accordance with Chapter 16. 
❖ 
In cases where play structures exceed 55 
square meter or are higher than 3 m, the 
structure shall be subject to structural load 
requirements of Chapter 16 and designed 
according to common engineering practice. 
SECTION 425 HYPERBARIC 
FACILITIES 
425.1  Hyperbaric 
facilities. 
Hyperbaric 
facilities shall meet the requirements contained in 
Chapter 14 of NFPA 99.  
❖ 
For these unique treatment facilities, Chapter 
20 of NFPA 99 provides appropriate design 
criteria. While hyperbaric facilities are often 
located in a hospital facility (Group I-2, 
Condition 2), they can also be found at 
independent medical facilities or ambulatory 
care facilities.  
SECTION 426 COMBUSTIBLE DUSTS, 
GRAIN PROCESSING AND STORAGE 
426.1  General. The provisions of Sections 
426.1.1 through 426.1.7 shall apply to buildings in 
which materials that produce combustible dusts are 
stored or handled. Buildings that store or handle 
combustible dusts shall comply with the applicable 
provisions of SBC 801. Where required by the fire 
code official, NFPA 652 and the applicable 
provisions of NFPA 61, NFPA 85, NFPA 120, 
NFPA 484, NFPA 654, NFPA 655 and NFPA 664 
shall apply.  
❖ 
Combustible dusts can generate high-pressure 
gas by combustion in air. The pressure created 
can destroy process equipment and structures. 
While there is a minimum concentration of dust 
required, 
there 
is 
no 
reliable 
upper 
concentration 
limitation 
beyond 
which 
combustion will not occur. The ignition source 
is often the process equipment itself. The 
provisions of this section primarily address the 
construction of a building that contains this 
hazard. The construction requirements are 
primarily intended to reduce the exposure 
hazard should a fire or explosion occur. To 
minimize the impact of the explosion on the 
structure, explosion control in accordance with 
Section 414.5.1 and SBC 801 is required. The 
provisions of this section apply to all buildings 
in which combustible dusts and particles are of 
-
